docs.title: Arcadia
docs.text:
Arcadia is a constructed language designed around regularity, transparent spelling, and grammatically meaningful sound patterns.

The language prioritises:

  • predictable orthography;
  • a compact vowel system;
  • controlled phonotactics;
  • regular derivation and inflection;
  • syntactic roles that are explicit rather than inferred from word order alone.

The documentation describes the current specification of the language. Some areas, especially vocabulary, idiom, and edge-case syntax, remain under active development.

docs.title: How to Use This Guide
docs.text:
For a first reading, use the following order:

  1. Read the Phonology and Orthography Guide.
  2. Read the Verbs Guide.
  3. Read the Nouns Guide.
  4. Review the Articles Guide.

The Vocabulary Guide is primarily a reference section. It does not need to be read linearly.

docs.title: Core Structure
docs.text:
Arcadia is built around a verb-centred syntax.

The standard clause order is verb-initial: the verb comes first, followed by its participant roles. Because the language uses explicit role marking and allows any recoverable role to be omitted, word order may shift for information structure, emphasis, or discourse flow.

Arcadia is influenced by Austronesian-style alignment, but does not implement a purely Austronesian system. Its grammar treats clause participants as roles licensed by the verb, rather than treating one participant role as structurally primary in every clause.

For the details of the case system, see the Noun Cases Guide. For verbal morphology and derived verb forms, see the Verb Generation Guide.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Many nominative-accusative systems can be described asymmetrically. In a basic active clause, the subject is structurally central, while objects are organised around the verb as complements.

graph LR
  A([**Subject**]) --> B@{ shape: circle, label: "**Action**" }
  subgraph objects
    direction TB
    C([**Direct Object**]) --> D([**Indirect Object**])
  end
  B --> objects

In a passive construction, one object may be promoted, but the structure of the clause changes.

graph LR
  A([**Promoted Object**]) --> B@{ shape: circle, label: "**Action**" }
  subgraph participants
    direction TB
    C([**Agent**]) --> D([**Recipient**])
  end
  B --> participants

docs.title: Arcadian Symmetry
docs.text:
Arcadian syntax is designed to be more symmetric. The verb remains the structural centre of the clause, and each participant is interpreted through the role assigned to it.

graph TB
  B@{ shape: circle, label: "**Action**" }
  subgraph participants
    direction LR
    A([**Donor**])
    C([**Asset**])
    D([**Recipient**])
  end
  B --> A
  B --> C
  B --> D

In this model, participant roles are not merely positional. A clause can foreground different roles without requiring a passive-style restructuring of the entire sentence.

docs.title: Secondary Clauses
docs.text:
Arcadia permits finite secondary clauses.

A secondary clause may contain a fully inflected verb rather than relying on a personless infinitive. Depending on the construction, the verb may still express mood, person, tense, and other verbal categories.

By default, tense in a secondary clause is interpreted from the narrator's temporal viewpoint, unless another rule or construction explicitly shifts that reference point.

Secondary clauses also interact with the phonological system through secondary devoicing, where relevant voiced verbal consonants surface as their voiceless counterparts in secondary environments.

docs.title: Development Roadmap
docs.text:
This roadmap records the current documentation and stabilisation targets for Arcadia.

Version Focus Main Criteria Status v0.2 Lexicon and aesthetic freeze Establish enough vocabulary to test the language in extended text. Stabilise the current sound system, phonotactics, and major spelling conventions. In Progress v0.3 Structural foundation freeze Finalise the major morphology and syntax rules, including noun cases, verbal categories, clause structure, and the abstract syntax model used by formal tooling. Planned v1.0 Functional usability release Complete the core documentation and resolve remaining high-impact edge cases. The language should be usable for complex communication within the documented system. Planned v2.0 Formal analysis and tooling Develop parser-facing grammar definitions and document stable allophonic or dialectal variation that emerges from use. Planned

docs.title: Orthography and Phonology
docs.text:
This section defines the native writing system, punctuation, sound inventory, stress system, and phonotactic rules of Arcadia.

Arcadian spelling is designed to be transparent: native graphemes correspond predictably to phonemes, and phonological alternations are reflected consistently in writing. The orthography is lowercase by default, avoids silent letters, and reserves non-native spellings for names, loanwords, symbols, and technical notation.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Contents Orthography Writing principles, character set, stress marking, vowel merging, loanwords, and normalisation. Letters Vowel and consonant graphemes, IPA values, digraphs, and gemination. Punctuation Sentence punctuation, quotation punctuation, and spacing conventions. Phonology Phonemic inventory, phonotactics, stress, vowel length, secondary devoicing, and marked consonants.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
Arcadian phonology follows five main principles:

  1. Small vowel system: Arcadia has five vowel phonemes: /i e a o u/.
  2. Predictable vowel length: the stressed vowel is phonetically long; vowel length is not independently contrastive.
  3. Symmetric voicing pairs: most obstruents occur in voiced and voiceless pairs, supporting regular grammatical alternations.
  4. Restricted marked sounds: /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ are not ordinary inherited root consonants; they primarily mark dubitative morphology and occur in loanwords.
  5. Soft but ancient sound profile: the language favors sonorants, controlled fricatives, dental endings, and limited clusters while avoiding excessive harshness.

docs.title: Notation
docs.text:
Notation Meaning / / Phonemic transcription. [ ] Phonetic transcription. ⟨ ⟩ Orthographic form or grapheme. C Any consonant. V Any vowel. R A liquid: /l/, /ʎ/ or /ɾ/. N A nasal: /m/, /n/, or /ɲ/. O An obstruent: plosive, fricative, or affricate.

Unless otherwise specified, phonological rules apply to native Arcadian vocabulary only.

docs.title: Orthography
docs.text: Arcadia uses a Latin-based orthography with predictable grapheme-to-phoneme correspondence. Native spelling is designed to be readable, regular, and compatible with plain ASCII input.

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
Native Arcadian orthography follows these principles:

  • each native grapheme has a predictable pronunciation;
  • there are no silent letters in native words;
  • native text is written in lowercase;
  • capitalisation is not used for native grammatical purposes;
  • foreign names, standardised symbols, and technical abbreviations may keep their source spelling;
  • stress is marked only when it is not penultimate;
  • vowel length is normally not written, because it is predictable from stress.

docs.title: Character Set
docs.text:
Arcadia uses the Latin letters below in native orthographic material:

a b c d e f g h i j l m n o p r s t u v x y z

The letters ⟨k⟩, ⟨q⟩, and ⟨w⟩ are not part of the native Arcadian character set.

Although ⟨h⟩, ⟨x⟩, and ⟨y⟩ belong to the character set, they are not independent native graphemes. In native spelling, they occur only as the second letter of a digraph.

docs.title: Independent Graphemes
docs.text:
The following letters function as independent native graphemes:

a b c d e f g i j l m n o p r s t u v z

The following letters do not function independently in native words:

h x y

docs.title: Digraphs
docs.text:
Arcadia uses digraphs for phonemes not represented by a single independent letter.

ch dh gh sh th zh dx tx ly ny

The digraphs are treated as single graphemes for pronunciation and phonological analysis.

docs.title: Stress Marking
docs.text:
Monosyllabic native words are stressed on their only syllable and do not require an accent mark.

In multisyllabic native words, penultimate stress is the default. Words with penultimate stress are written without a stress mark.

Words with non-penultimate stress take an acute accent on the stressed vowel:

á é í ó ú

The acute accent marks stress only. It does not mark vowel quality.

docs.title: Stress and Vowel Length
docs.text:
The stressed vowel is phonetically long. Unstressed vowels are phonetically short.

Because vowel length is predictable from stress, vowel length is not normally written in the orthography.

In phonetic transcription, stressed vowels may be written with the length mark [ː]. In phonemic or orthographic discussion, length is normally omitted unless the distinction between phonemic and phonetic representation is relevant.

docs.title: Vowel Merging
docs.text:
Adjacent identical vowels merge into a single long vowel in careful grammatical description.

A macron may be used in dictionaries, paradigms, or grammatical references to show a merged long vowel:

ā ē ī ō ū

The macron is not part of ordinary running text. In ordinary spelling, merged vowels may be written in simplified form unless a grammatical reference requires the merger to be shown explicitly.

docs.title: Gemination
docs.text:
Consonant gemination is written by doubling the relevant consonant letter.

For single-letter graphemes, the letter is doubled.

For digraphs, only the final letter of the digraph is doubled. The first letter remains unchanged so that the base digraph remains recognisable.

docs.title: Native and Non-Native Spelling
docs.text:
Native Arcadian words follow native orthographic restrictions.

Proper names, international names, standardised symbols, scientific units, and technical abbreviations may retain non-native characters, capitalisation, or spelling conventions.

When a non-native spelling is retained, pronunciation may be supplied with IPA where clarity is needed.

docs.title: Orthographic Normalisation
docs.text:
For normalised native text:

  • use lowercase letters;
  • use native graphemes only;
  • omit capitalisation;
  • mark only non-penultimate stress;
  • do not write predictable vowel length;
  • do not use standalone ⟨h⟩, ⟨x⟩, or ⟨y⟩ in native words;
  • reserve ⟨k⟩, ⟨q⟩, and ⟨w⟩ for non-native material only.

docs.title: Letters
docs.text: This file defines Arcadian letters, digraphs, and their phonemic values.

docs.title: Vowels
docs.text:
Arcadia has five vowel phonemes:

/i e a o u/

Each vowel can occur in stressed and unstressed syllables. Vowel quality remains stable across stress positions, though the stressed vowel is phonetically long.

Height Front Central Back Close /i/ /u/ Mid /e/ /o/ Open /a/

docs.title: Vowel Graphemes
docs.text: Grapheme Phoneme ⟨i⟩ /i/ ⟨e⟩ /e/ ⟨a⟩ /a/ ⟨o⟩ /o/ ⟨u⟩ /u/

docs.title: Consonant Inventory
docs.text:
All Arcadian consonants are pulmonic.

Manner Bilabial Labiodental Dental Alveolar Post-Alveolar Palatal Velar Nasal /m/ /n/ /ɲ/ Plosive /p b/ /t d/ /k g/ Sibilant fricative /s z/ /ʃ ʒ/ Non-sibilant fricative /f v/ /θ ð/ /x ɣ/ Affricate /t͡ʃ d͡ʒ/ Tap/flap /ɾ/ Approximant /j/ Lateral approximant /l/ /ʎ/

docs.title: Single-Letter Consonant Graphemes
docs.text: Grapheme Phoneme ⟨b⟩ /b/ ⟨c⟩ /k/ ⟨d⟩ /d/ ⟨f⟩ /f/ ⟨g⟩ /g/ ⟨j⟩ /j/ ⟨l⟩ /l/ ⟨m⟩ /m/ ⟨n⟩ /n/ ⟨p⟩ /p/ ⟨r⟩ /ɾ/ ⟨s⟩ /s/ ⟨t⟩ /t/ ⟨v⟩ /v/ ⟨z⟩ /z/

docs.title: Digraphs
docs.text: Grapheme Phoneme Status ⟨ch⟩ /x/ Native digraph. ⟨dh⟩ /ð/ Native digraph. ⟨gh⟩ /ɣ/ Native digraph. ⟨sh⟩ /ʃ/ Native digraph. ⟨th⟩ /θ/ Native digraph. ⟨zh⟩ /ʒ/ Native digraph. ⟨dx⟩ /d͡ʒ/ Marked grammatical digraph; also permitted in loanwords. ⟨tx⟩ /t͡ʃ/ Marked grammatical digraph; also permitted in loanwords. ⟨ly⟩ /ʎ/ Native digraph. ⟨ny⟩ /ɲ/ Native digraph.

docs.title: Voicing Pairs
docs.text:
The core obstruent inventory is organised into voiced and voiceless pairs.

Voiceless Voiced /p/ ⟨p⟩ /b/ ⟨b⟩ /t/ ⟨t⟩ /d/ ⟨d⟩ /k/ ⟨c⟩ /g/ ⟨g⟩ /f/ ⟨f⟩ /v/ ⟨v⟩ /θ/ ⟨th⟩ /ð/ ⟨dh⟩ /s/ ⟨s⟩ /z/ ⟨z⟩ /ʃ/ ⟨sh⟩ /ʒ/ ⟨zh⟩ /x/ ⟨ch⟩ /ɣ/ ⟨gh⟩ /t͡ʃ/ ⟨tx⟩ /d͡ʒ/ ⟨dx⟩

docs.title: Sonorants
docs.text:
Sonorants do not participate in secondary devoicing.

Class Phonemes Graphemes Nasals /m n ɲ/ ⟨m n ny⟩ Liquids /l ɾ ʎ/ ⟨l r ly⟩ Approximant /j/ ⟨j⟩

docs.title: Marked Affricates
docs.text:
The affricates /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ are not ordinary inherited root consonants.

In native grammar, /d͡ʒ/ is associated with the dubitative mood, and /t͡ʃ/ is its secondary-clause devoiced counterpart.

Outside this grammatical function, ⟨tx⟩ and ⟨dx⟩ are permitted mainly in loanwords, names, or explicit phonetic transcription.

docs.title: Gemination
docs.text:
Gemination is represented orthographically by doubling.

For single-letter graphemes, the consonant letter is doubled.

For digraphs, only the final letter is doubled:

Base Type Gemination Rule single-letter consonant double the consonant letter digraph ending in ⟨h⟩ double ⟨h⟩ digraph ending in ⟨x⟩ double ⟨x⟩ digraph ending in ⟨y⟩ double ⟨y⟩

Gemination is phonemic where specified by the lexicon or morphology.

docs.title: Punctuation
docs.text: Arcadian punctuation is conservative and functional. It marks sentence type, clause structure, quotation, and list structure without interacting with capitalisation.

docs.title: Punctuation Marks
docs.text: Mark Name Usage . Period Ends a declarative sentence. ; Semicolon Connects closely related independent clauses or separates complex list items. , Comma Separates list items, non-final clauses, parenthetical material, and light syntactic breaks. : Colon Introduces an explanation, quotation, formal statement, list, or expansion. ! Exclamation mark Marks strong emotion, urgency, emphasis, or command force. ? Question mark Marks a direct question. "" Quotation marks Enclose direct speech, quoted material, titles, or cited forms.

docs.title: Spacing
docs.text:
In normalised text:

  • no space appears before a punctuation mark;
  • one space appears after sentence-final punctuation;
  • one space appears after commas, semicolons, and colons;
  • quotation marks are placed directly beside the material they enclose.

docs.title: Sentence-Final Punctuation
docs.text:
A sentence normally takes exactly one final punctuation mark.

When quoted material occurs at the end of a sentence, duplicated final punctuation is avoided. If the quoted material and the surrounding sentence would end with the same punctuation mark, the mark is written once, outside the quotation.

If the quoted material requires a different punctuation mark from the surrounding sentence, the mark belonging to the quoted material remains inside the quotation, and the mark belonging to the surrounding sentence remains outside.

docs.title: Comma
docs.text:
The comma marks a minor syntactic boundary. It may separate:

  • coordinated items;
  • secondary and other dependent clauses;
  • introductory material;
  • parenthetical phrases;
  • vocative elements;
  • appositional material.

Commas should not be used where a stronger boundary is required.

docs.title: Semicolon
docs.text:
The semicolon marks a boundary stronger than a comma but weaker than a period.

It may join independent clauses when the second clause continues, contrasts with, or clarifies the first. It may also separate list items that are internally complex.

docs.title: Colon
docs.text:
The colon introduces material that expands, explains, defines, enumerates, or formally presents what precedes it.

It may introduce quoted speech, but quotation marks are still used when the quoted material is direct.

docs.title: Question Mark
docs.text:
The question mark marks direct questions.

Indirect questions do not require a question mark unless the full sentence is itself interrogative.

docs.title: Exclamation Mark
docs.text:
The exclamation mark marks heightened force.

It should be used sparingly in formal or neutral prose.

docs.title: Quotation Marks
docs.text:
Double quotation marks are the default quotation marks.

Quotation marks may enclose:

  • direct speech;
  • cited wording;
  • titles;
  • orthographic forms under discussion;
  • metalinguistic references.

For linguistic notation, IPA and orthographic brackets are preferred where precision is needed.

docs.title: Capitalisation
docs.text:
Native Arcadian punctuation does not trigger capitalisation.

Sentence beginnings, names, titles, and quoted material remain lowercase in normalised native text unless non-native spelling conventions are intentionally retained.

docs.title: Phonology
docs.text: This file defines the phonemic inventory, stress system, phonotactics, and major morphophonological alternations of Arcadia.

docs.title: Phonemic Vowels
docs.text:
Arcadia has five vowel phonemes:

/i e a o u/
Height Front Central Back Close /i/ /u/ Mid /e/ /o/ Open /a/

Vowel quality is stable across stressed and unstressed syllables.

docs.title: Stress and Vowel Length
docs.text:
Stress is phonologically significant.

The stressed vowel is phonetically long:

/V/ → [Vː] / stressed syllable

Unstressed vowels are phonetically short.

Vowel length is therefore predictable from stress and is not an independent phonemic contrast.

docs.title: Default Stress
docs.text:
Monosyllabic words are stressed on their only syllable.

In multisyllabic words, stress defaults to the penultimate syllable.

Non-penultimate stress is marked orthographically with an acute accent on the stressed vowel.

docs.title: Consonant Inventory
docs.text: Manner Bilabial Labiodental Dental Alveolar Post-Alveolar Palatal Velar Nasal /m/ /n/ /ɲ/ Plosive /p b/ /t d/ /k g/ Sibilant fricative /s z/ /ʃ ʒ/ Non-sibilant fricative /f v/ /θ ð/ /x ɣ/ Affricate /t͡ʃ d͡ʒ/ Tap/flap /ɾ/ Approximant /j/ Lateral approximant /l/ /ʎ/

docs.title: Obstruent Pairing
docs.text:
Most obstruents are organised into voicing pairs.

Voiceless Voiced /p/ /b/ /t/ /d/ /k/ /g/ /f/ /v/ /θ/ /ð/ /s/ /z/ /ʃ/ /ʒ/ /x/ /ɣ/ /t͡ʃ/ /d͡ʒ/

This pairing supports secondary devoicing and other regular morphophonological alternations.

docs.title: Sonorants
docs.text:
The sonorants are:

/m n ɲ l ʎ ɾ j/

Sonorants do not undergo secondary devoicing.

docs.title: Marked Velar Fricatives
docs.text:
The velar fricatives /x/ and /ɣ/ are part of the phonemic inventory but should remain relatively marked in inherited vocabulary.

The voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ is preferred in sonorous environments, especially between vowels or near liquids.

The voiceless velar fricative /x/ is stronger and should be used more sparingly in high-register native roots. It is also the regular secondary-devoiced counterpart of /ɣ/.

docs.title: Marked Affricates
docs.text:
The affricates /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ are highly restricted.

They do not occur freely in inherited lexical roots.

The voiced affricate /d͡ʒ/ marks the dubitative mood. The voiceless affricate /t͡ʃ/ is its secondary-clause devoiced counterpart.

Outside dubitative morphology, these affricates are permitted primarily in loanwords, names, and explicit transcription.

docs.title: Basic Syllable Structure
docs.text:
The basic syllable template is:

(C)(R/J)V(C)(C)

Where:

  • C is a consonant;
  • R is a liquid, /l/, /ʎ/ or /ɾ/;
  • J is /j/;
  • V is a vowel.

The preferred syllable shapes are:

CV
CVC
CCV
CCVC

The following shapes are permitted but less central:

V
VC
CVCC

No native syllable has more than two consonants in the onset or coda.

docs.title: Onsets
docs.text:
Single-consonant onsets are broadly permitted.

The affricates /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ are restricted by morphology and loanword status rather than by ordinary onset phonotactics.

The phoneme /ɣ/ is permitted as an onset but is preferred medially or in sonorous environments.

docs.title: Onset Clusters
docs.text:
Native onset clusters are limited.

Preferred onset clusters consist of an obstruent followed by a liquid:

OR

A more restricted set of obstruent + /j/ clusters is also permitted:

Oj

Onset clusters involving /x/, /ɣ/, /t͡ʃ/, or /d͡ʒ/ are disfavored in inherited vocabulary.

Three-consonant onsets are not permitted in native words.

docs.title: Codas
docs.text:
Codas are more restricted than onsets.

Preferred codas are sonorants and voiceless fricatives:

/m n ɲ l ʎ ɾ f θ s ʃ x/

Voiceless stops are permitted as stronger codas:

/p t k/

Voiced obstruents are normally avoided word-finally:

/b d g v ð z ʒ ɣ d͡ʒ/

The final dental fricative /θ/ is a preferred high-register ending and is especially compatible with archaic, formal, or secondary morphology.

docs.title: Final Devoicing
docs.text:
Word-final obstruents are normally voiceless in native phonology.

Non-final voiced obstruent Word-final voiceless counterpart /b/ /p/ /d/ /t/ /g/ /k/ /v/ /f/ /ð/ /θ/ /z/ /s/ /ʒ/ /ʃ/ /ɣ/ /x/ /d͡ʒ/ /t͡ʃ/

Final devoicing is the default phonological outcome for native words. However, morphology may explicitly preserve a final voiced obstruent where it carries grammatical meaning.

The fractional number is one such example. It is realised with a final /d/, which remains voiced despite occurring word-finally. This preserves the morphological identity of the fractional number and gives it a distinct phonetic character.

Foreign names, technical terms, and careful citation forms may also preserve final voiced obstruents.

docs.title: Medial Clusters
docs.text:
Medial clusters may be broader than onset or coda clusters, especially across morpheme boundaries.

Preferred medial clusters follow one of these patterns:

RC
NC
OR
Oj

Where:

  • R is /l/, /ʎ/ or /ɾ/;
  • N is /m/, /n/, or /ɲ/;
  • O is an obstruent;
  • C is any consonant;
  • j is /j/.

Clusters with more than two consonants are avoided in native roots and should arise only through morphology, compounding, or non-native material.

docs.title: Sonority
docs.text:
Native syllables generally rise in sonority toward the vowel and fall in sonority after it.

Preferred onsets move from less sonorous to more sonorous material. Preferred codas move from more sonorous to less sonorous material or end in a single sonorant.

This principle may be overridden at morpheme boundaries, but inherited roots should remain sonority-compliant.

docs.title: Hiatus
docs.text:
Roots avoid vowel hiatus.

Across morpheme boundaries, adjacent vowels may be resolved by:

  • glide insertion after front vowels;
  • glide insertion after back vowels;
  • contraction of identical vowels;
  • preservation of hiatus in careful or poetic speech.

Identical vowel contraction may be shown with a macron in dictionaries or grammatical references.

docs.title: Gemination
docs.text:
Consonant gemination is phonemic where specified by the lexicon or morphology.

Gemination may occur medially in native words.

Geminated affricates are avoided except in loanwords, expressive forms, or technical transcription.

docs.title: Secondary Devoicing
docs.text:
Secondary devoicing is a grammatical morphophonological process affecting verbs in secondary clauses.

In the affected verbal domain, voiced obstruents become their voiceless counterparts.

Primary voiced consonant Secondary devoiced consonant /b/ /p/ /d/ /t/ /g/ /k/ /v/ /f/ /ð/ /θ/ /z/ /s/ /ʒ/ /ʃ/ /ɣ/ /x/ /d͡ʒ/ /t͡ʃ/

Secondary devoicing does not affect vowels, sonorants, stress, or predictable stress-based vowel length.

The grammatical scope of secondary devoicing is defined by the verbal morphology. Phonologically, the output must respect the ordinary voiceless counterpart of each affected obstruent.

docs.title: Dubitative Mood
docs.text:
The dubitative mood is marked by /d͡ʒ/.

In secondary clauses, dubitative /d͡ʒ/ undergoes secondary devoicing to /t͡ʃ/.

This makes /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ part of the regular voicing architecture while keeping them rare in ordinary lexical material.

docs.title: Loanwords and Names
docs.text:
Loanwords and names may contain phonological or orthographic material outside the native system.

When naturalised, loanwords should be adapted to native grapheme-phoneme correspondence and native phonotactics.

When not naturalised, they may retain source spelling, capitalisation, and non-native letters.

docs.title: Phonological Style
docs.text:
Arcadian inherited vocabulary should prefer:

  • sonorants over dense stop clusters;
  • dental and alveolar fricatives over harsh gutturals;
  • limited, strategic use of /x/ and /ɣ/;
  • rare use of /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ outside their grammatical role;
  • predictable stress and stress-based vowel length;
  • moderate syllable weight rather than extreme consonant density.

docs.title: Verbs

docs.title: Nouns

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Arcadian syntax is verb-first and role-based. The verb functions as the structural core of the clause: it licenses participant roles and determines which roles are mandatory or optional.

Arcadia does not categorise verbs by transitivity. Valency emerges from verb type and role structure, not from a transitive/intransitive distinction.

docs.title: Clause Structure
docs.text:
The default clause order is:

Verb – Core Roles – Obliques

Core roles and obliques may be reordered for discourse emphasis, poetic rhythm, or stylistic effect.

Role assignment is never determined by word order. Roles must remain recoverable through morphology and verb type.

Arcadia is fully pro-drop. Core roles may be omitted when recoverable from verbal morphology or discourse context.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Each verb type defines a set of participant roles. Some roles are mandatory, while others are optional.

Arcadia is pivotless. No participant is grammatically privileged over the others, and no role is promoted to control the clause. Participant roles are identified directly through semantic case marking.

In all verb types, the verb comes first in the sentence structure and is followed by its assigned roles. Core roles follow in the canonical order defined by the verb type, first the mandatory ones, and finally the optional ones. Case marking allows roles to be reordered when the intended structure remains recoverable.

docs.title: Verb Types
docs.text:
Verb types and their role structures are defined in Verb Types.

The current system distinguishes four core verb families:

  • existential verbs;
  • relational verbs;
  • mental verbs;
  • agentive verbs.

These families may contain more specific subtypes.

docs.title: Syntax Sections
docs.text:
• Verb Types
  • Conditionals
  • Prepositions

docs.title: Verb Types
docs.text:
In Arcadia, verb type determines clause structure. The verb functions as the structural core of the clause: it licenses participant roles and determines which roles are mandatory or optional.

Arcadia does not categorise verbs by transitivity. Valency emerges from verb type and role structure, not from a transitive–intransitive distinction.

Arcadia is pivotless. No participant is grammatically privileged over the others, and no role is promoted to control the clause. Participant roles are identified directly through semantic case marking.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
A role is a participant position licensed by a verb. Each clause has at most one verb, and each verb type defines which roles may appear with that verb.

Roles are divided into two groups:

  • Mandatory roles are required by the verb's role pattern.
  • Optional roles may appear when the clause needs them, but they are not required for the verb to form a complete clause.

A mandatory role may still be absent from the surface clause. It can be omitted when agreement or context makes it recoverable, or it can be overloaded when the verb itself supplies that part of the meaning.

For example, "Maria kicked the ball on Sunday" contains one verb, "kicked". The verb licenses two core roles: "Maria" as the agent and "the ball" as the patient. The phrase "on Sunday" is not a core participant role; it is an optional time expression.

"Kicked" alone does not form a complete clause in this use. "Maria kicked the ball" does, because the mandatory roles licensed by the verb have been assigned.

docs.title: Overloading
docs.text:
Overloading occurs when a verb contains one or more roles inside its lexical meaning. An overloaded role is still part of the verb's meaning, but it cannot be assigned independently in the clause.

Overloading is different from omission. When a role is omitted, the role is still recoverable from agreement, morphology, or discourse context. When a role is overloaded, the verb itself supplies that part of the meaning.

For example, a liminal verb normally licenses an entity, an origin, and a destination. A clause can assign those roles explicitly:

The ice changed from a solid into a liquid.

Here, "the ice" is the entity, "from a solid" is the origin, and "into a liquid" is the destination.

The verb "melt" is different. In "The ice melted", the change from solid to liquid is built into the verb. The origin and destination are overloaded, so they are not assigned as independent roles.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Arcadia distinguishes four core verb types:

  • Existential verbs
    • Stative verbs
    • Liminal verbs
  • Relational verbs
    • Relationship verbs
    • Transfer verbs
  • Mental verbs
    • Experiential verbs
    • Cognitive verbs
  • Agentive verbs

In all verb types, the verb comes first in the clause. Core roles follow in the canonical order defined by the verb type, followed by optional expressions. Case marking allows roles to be reordered when the intended structure remains recoverable.

docs.title: Existential Verbs
docs.text:
Existential verbs describe being, states, locations, movement, natural phenomena, and changes of state. They are divided into two subtypes: stative verbs and liminal verbs.

graph TB
  A@{ shape: circle, label: "**Existential**<br/>_Became_" }
  subgraph participants
    direction LR
    B([**Entity**<br/>_the sky_])
    C([**Origin**<br/>_from grey_])
    D([**Destination**<br/>_blue_])
  end
  A --> B
  A --> C
  A --> D

docs.title: Stative Verbs
docs.text:
Stative verbs describe states, properties, locations, movement, existence, or natural phenomena.

Roles:

  • Entity: the thing that exists, moves, or has the state.
  • State: the state, property, location, movement, or phenomenon.

Canonical order: Verb - Entity - State

Overloading: Stative verbs can be totally overloaded. When a stative verb is totally overloaded, no role is assigned in the clause and the verb drops personal and number marking. Natural-phenomenon verbs such as weather expressions are typical examples.

docs.title: Liminal Verbs
docs.text:
Liminal verbs describe transitions or changes of state.

Roles:

  • Entity: the thing undergoing the transition.
  • Origin: the starting point or previous state.
  • Destination: the endpoint or resulting state.

Canonical order: Verb - Entity - Origin - Destination

Overloading: Liminal verbs often overload the origin, the destination, or both. For example, "melt" includes the change from solid to liquid, so those endpoints are not assigned separately.

docs.title: Relational Verbs
docs.text:
Relational verbs describe relationships, possession, and changes in who has or controls something. They are divided into two subtypes: relationship verbs and transfer verbs.

graph TB
  A@{ shape: circle, label: "**Relational**<br/>_Bought_" }
  subgraph participants
    direction LR
    B([**Donor**<br/>_from John_])
    C([**Recipient**<br/>_Mary_])
    D([**Asset**<br/>_the ball_])
    E([**Compensation**<br/>_for a thousand pounds_])
  end
  A --> B
  A --> C
  A --> D
  A --> E

docs.title: Relationship Verbs
docs.text:
Relationship verbs describe relationships or possession.

Roles:

  • Relator: the participant from whose perspective the relationship is framed.
  • Correlate: the other participant or thing in the relationship.
  • Relationship: the relation that connects them.

Canonical order: Verb - Relator - Correlate - Relationship

Overloading: Relationship verbs are often relationship-overloaded. For example, a possession verb may contain the relationship meaning "have", so the clause assigns the relator and correlate without separately assigning the relationship itself.

docs.title: Transfer Verbs
docs.text:
Transfer verbs describe a change of relationship through giving, receiving, selling, buying, or transfer.

Roles:

  • Donor: the participant from whom the asset moves.
  • Recipient: the participant to whom the asset moves.
  • Asset: the thing transferred.
  • Compensation: the payment, exchange, or return value.

Canonical order: Verb - Donor - Recipient - Asset - Compensation

Overloading: Transfer verbs may overload parts of the transfer or exchange structure. Perspective does not change the syntactic status of any participant: donor, recipient, asset, and compensation remain equally identified by their semantic roles.

docs.title: Mental Verbs
docs.text:
Mental verbs describe experience, perception, emotion, knowledge, thought, and mental content. They are divided into two subtypes: experiential verbs and cognitive verbs.

graph TB
  A@{ shape: circle, label: "**Mental**<br/>_Loves_" }
  subgraph participants
    direction LR
    B([**Experiencer**<br/>_Maria_])
    C([**Stimulus**<br/>_football_])
  end
  A --> B
  A --> C

docs.title: Experiential Verbs
docs.text:
Experiential verbs describe preferences, sensations, or emotions.

Roles:

  • Experiencer: the participant having the feeling, sensation, or preference.
  • Stimulus: the thing that causes, receives, or defines the experience.

Canonical order: Verb - Experiencer - Stimulus

Overloading: Experiential verbs may overload the stimulus when the experience is lexicalised as a general state, sensation, or condition.

docs.title: Cognitive Verbs
docs.text:
Cognitive verbs describe knowledge, thought, dreaming, memory, belief, or other mental content.

Roles:

  • Cogniser: the participant who knows, thinks, remembers, dreams, or believes.
  • Concept: the thought, knowledge, memory, belief, or content.

Canonical order: Verb - Cogniser - Concept

Overloading: Cognitive verbs may overload the concept when the verb names a specific mental activity whose content is not independently assigned.

docs.title: Agentive Verbs
docs.text:
Agentive verbs describe actions, communication, interaction, and events. They are not divided into subtypes in the current system.

graph TB
  A@{ shape: circle, label: "**Agentive**<br/>_Kicked_" }
  subgraph participants
    direction LR
    B([**Agent**<br/>_Mary_])
    C([**Patient**<br/>_the ball_])
    D([**Recipient**<br/>_to John_])
  end
  A --> B
  A --> C
  A --> D

Roles:

  • Agent: the participant carrying out or initiating the action.
  • Patient: the participant affected by the action.
  • Recipient: the participant towards whom the action, message, or result is directed.

Canonical order: Verb - Agent - Patient - Recipient

Overloading: Agentive verbs may overload the patient or recipient when the affected participant or endpoint is included in the verb's meaning.

docs.title: Phrase Heads and Case Marking
docs.text:
Arcadia uses a hybrid case-marking system designed for deterministic parsing.

Every phrase has a head. A grammatical marker attaches to the head of the phrase, while its scope extends over the entire phrase. Its realisation depends on the lexical category of that head.

When the head is a noun or an adjective functioning nominally, the case marker attaches directly to it and surfaces as an inflectional case ending. When the head belongs to a category that does not inflect for case, such as a preposition or verb, the same marker is realised as a detached clitic.

A coordinated phrase has a compound head. Each conjunct contributes a head to the complete phrase, and the case marker is realised on every conjunct head. The conjunction binds the marked conjuncts into one syntactic unit.

Adjectives do not inherit the case of the noun phrase they modify. They agree only with the grammatical class of their nominal head. This keeps the phrase head overtly identifiable.

An adjective may itself head a noun phrase. In that use, it receives case marking directly:

  • the pregnant woman: the adjective modifies a case-marked noun;
  • the pregnant: the adjective is the case-marked head of the noun phrase.

Case therefore identifies both the semantic role of a phrase and the overt head through which that phrase participates in the clause.

Arcadian clauses have no pivot. Each participant is marked directly according to the semantic role licensed by the verb, and no role receives a privileged nominative status.

Type Role Case Stative Entity Thematic Stative State Essive Liminal Entity Thematic Liminal Origin Ablative Liminal Destination Allative Relationship Relator Thematic Relationship Correlate Comitative Relationship Relationship Essive Transfer Asset Thematic Transfer Donor Ablative Transfer Recipient Allative Transfer Compensation Benefactive Experiential Experiencer Experiential Experiential Stimulus Stimulative Cognitive Cogniser Cognitive Cognitive Concept Conceptual Agentive Agent Ergative Agentive Patient Accusative Agentive Recipient Dative

The names of the four mental-role cases remain provisional. Their defining property is that they mark their semantic roles directly rather than deriving from pivot selection.

docs.title: Reflexivity and Reciprocity
docs.text:
Arcadia uses prefixes to mark reflexive and reciprocal constructions.

Prefix scope order is fixed:

Reflexive / Reciprocal -> other derivational prefixes -> Root

The reflexive or reciprocal marker has the tightest role-changing scope among prefixes. Full forms and combinatorial rules are described in the verb word-formation guide.

docs.title: Verbs
docs.text:
Arcadian verbs encode grammatical information through a fixed and ordered morphological structure.

This section describes the forms of verbs: person, tense, aspect, mood, and the full verbal template. Clause structure, participant roles, and verb types are described in the Syntax guide and the Verb Types section.

docs.title: Verbal Morphology
docs.text:
Arcadia verbs encode grammatical information through a fixed suffix sequence.

The suffix template is:

Root–T–M–A–P/N

Where:

  • T (Tense): single vowel
  • M (Mood / Hypermood): one or two consonants
  • A (Aspect): vowel–consonant pair, or ∅ for zero aspect
  • P/N (Person/Number): one or two syllables.

Each slot contains at most one morpheme. Zero aspect is expressed by the absence of the aspect slot.

Detailed paradigms are provided in:

  • Person
  • Tense
  • Aspect
  • Mood
  • Conjugation

docs.title: Verb Type and Agreement
docs.text:
Verb type determines which participant roles are licensed by the verb. It also determines which roles are mandatory or optional, and how agreement and omission behave.

Arcadia does not categorise verbs by transitivity. Valency emerges from verb type and role structure, not from a transitive/intransitive distinction.

The role structures associated with each verb type are defined in Verb Types.

docs.title: Prefix Domain
docs.text:
Arcadia uses prefixes to mark role- and valency-related operations, including reflexive and reciprocal constructions.

Because Arcadia is pivotless, no prefix is needed to promote a participant: each role is identified directly through semantic case marking. This design:

  • avoids lexical verb pairs such as buy/sell-type alternations;
  • enables omission of any recoverable role;
  • supports the role-based alignment without duplicating roots.

Prefix scope order is currently defined as:

Reflexive / Reciprocal → other derivational prefixes → Root

The reflexive or reciprocal marker has the tightest role-changing scope among prefixes. Full prefix inventories and generation rules are described in the verb word-formation guide.
